title: Crémant Rosé — Victorine de Chastenay
quickDescription: Dry, bright, and festive — an easy way to begin the evening.
opening: A sparkling rosé that feels playful on first sip and quietly elegant by the second.
colorDesc: Pale salmon pink with a fine, cheerful bead.
nose: Red berries, citrus peel, and a whisper of brioche.
palate: Dry and bright with delicate fruit and lively bubbles.
finish: Fresh, lifted, and just a little mischievous.
place: Bourgogne is better known for still wines, but its sparkling expressions carry the same sense of restraint and elegance. Cool conditions preserve freshness while the traditional method adds texture and depth.
producer: Victorine de Chastenay focuses on classic French sparkle: balanced fruit, clean acidity, and bubbles that feel refined rather than flashy.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one rosé sparkler that felt fun without losing its sense of place. It brings charm immediately, but still feels polished enough for a slower, more thoughtful pour.
note: We wanted one rosé sparkler that felt fun without losing its sense of place. This one brings charm immediately, but it still feels polished enough for a slower, more thoughtful pour.
pairings: Fresh berries, soft cheese, and salty snacks
The glass you order when the table needs a lift
An easy beginning to a long evening
final: A sparkling rosé with both wit and poise.

title: Blanc de Blancs — Raventós i Blanc
quickDescription: Crisp and precise, with fine bubbles and a clean, refreshing finish.
opening: A wine that feels like celebration without the occasion—bright, effortless, and quietly joyful.
colorDesc: Pale straw with a constant stream of fine bubbles.
nose: Citrus zest, fresh bread, and green apple.
palate: Clean and lively with a soft creaminess beneath the bubbles.
place: Cava is Spain's answer to Champagne, made in Catalunya using the traditional method. Warm Mediterranean days and cool nights help preserve acidity while allowing the grapes to fully ripen.
producer: Raventós i Blanc is part of a long tradition of sparkling winemaking in the region, balancing freshness with texture and making wines that feel both festive and grounded.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a sparkling wine that did not feel reserved for a special moment. This one has just enough depth to keep you interested while still feeling easy, bright, and social.
note: We wanted a sparkling wine that did not feel reserved for a special moment. This one has just enough depth to keep you interested while still feeling easy, bright, and social.
pairings: Salted almonds, olives, and anything crisp and savory
The first glass of the evening
A toast that was never planned
final: A reminder that not every celebration needs a reason.

title: Prima-Secco — Shabo
quickDescription: Tart, layered, and more intriguing than expected — a sparkling conversation starter.
opening: A sparkling wine with an edge to it—tart, layered, and more intriguing than expected.
colorDesc: Pale straw with flashes of silver and a brisk mousse.
nose: White peach, citrus peel, and orchard blossom.
palate: Tart and lively with a slightly savory complexity beneath the fruit.
place: Shabo sits near the Black Sea, where maritime influence helps preserve freshness and shape aromatic whites and sparkling wines. It is a region with deep roots and a distinct identity.
producer: Shabo works with both local and international grapes, bringing precision to a wine culture that many guests may be discovering for the first time.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one bottle on the sparkling list that felt like a conversation starter. It is bright and fun, but it also introduces people to a place and a grape they likely have never met before.
note: We wanted one bottle on the sparkling list that felt like a conversation starter. This is it. It is bright and fun, but it also introduces people to a place and a grape they likely have never met before.
pairings: Olives, pickled things, and salty boards
The bottle for curious guests
A sparkler that turns into a story
final: A little unexpected—in exactly the right way.

title: Sancerre — Pierre Martin
quickDescription: Mineral and precise — the kind of white that rewards paying attention.
opening: A wine that feels like standing on cool stone after a long summer day—quiet, mineral, and alive with precision.
colorDesc: Pale green with silver reflections.
nose: Fresh citrus peel, crushed herbs, and wet stone.
palate: Lean and focused with bright acidity and a clean mineral core.
place: Sancerre sits high above the Loire River in central France, where limestone-rich soils and cool air shape wines of precision and clarity.
producer: Pierre Martin represents a classic, focused style of Sancerre winemaking: minimal intervention, bright acidity, and a strong sense of place.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a white that felt clean—not just in flavor, but in intention. This stood out immediately. It is precise, refreshing, and just serious enough to keep you paying attention.
note: We wanted a white that felt clean—not just in flavor, but in intention. This stood out immediately. It is precise, refreshing, and just serious enough to keep you paying attention.
pairings: Fresh chèvre, olives, and anything bright or herbal
A simple board and a quiet table
Best enjoyed slowly, between pages of a good book
final: A wine for paying attention—without needing to think too hard.

title: Sauvignon Blanc "Coeur du Mont" — Famille Dubard
quickDescription: Lively and citrusy, with the kind of freshness that wakes up your palate.
opening: A Sauvignon Blanc that feels vivid and generous—citrus-driven, clean, and effortlessly inviting.
colorDesc: Bright pale gold with green-tinged edges.
nose: Grapefruit, lime zest, fresh-cut grass, and a hint of white peach.
palate: Lively and citrus-forward with crisp acidity and a clean, juicy core.
finish: Bright, refreshing, and lingering with a subtle mineral lift.
place: The hillsides near Bordeaux produce whites with both ripeness and freshness—warmer than the Loire, but still shaped by Atlantic breezes and limestone soils.
producer: Famille Dubard has worked their vineyards for generations, crafting Sauvignon Blanc that feels both rooted in tradition and alive with freshness. Their "Coeur du Mont" bottling captures the best of the slope.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a Sauvignon Blanc that felt generous without losing its edge. This one wakes up the palate immediately and keeps things moving—exactly the kind of energy a wine list needs early in the white section.
note: We wanted a Sauvignon Blanc that felt generous without losing its edge. This one wakes up the palate immediately and keeps things moving—exactly the kind of energy a wine list needs early in the white section.
pairings: Fresh goat cheese, citrus-dressed salads, and herb boards
A white for guests who love brightness and energy
Perfect first glass of the evening
final: A white that greets you with sunshine.

title: Old Vine Chenin Blanc — Myburgh Bros
quickDescription: Textured and vibrant — generous fruit with real energy underneath.
opening: A Chenin Blanc with both depth and lift—fruit-forward, textured, and carrying the quiet authority of old vines.
colorDesc: Golden straw with a warm, luminous glow.
nose: Ripe stone fruit, honey, beeswax, and a whisper of chamomile.
palate: Generous and textured with concentrated fruit, bright acidity, and a waxy mid-palate richness.
finish: Long, layered, and gently honeyed with a clean, fresh exit.
place: Paarl sits in the heart of South Africa's winelands, where old Chenin Blanc vines—some planted decades ago—dig deep into granite-flecked soils. The result is concentration and character that young vines simply cannot match.
producer: Myburgh Bros at Joostenberg has farmed these vineyards for generations. Their old-vine Chenin Blanc captures the depth and personality that comes from patient farming and deep roots.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a Chenin Blanc that showed what old vines can do—wine with real weight and texture, but still vibrant enough to feel alive. This bottle delivers generosity without losing its edge.
note: We wanted a Chenin Blanc that showed what old vines can do—wine with real weight and texture, but still vibrant enough to feel alive. This bottle delivers generosity without losing its edge.
pairings: Aged cheese, roasted almonds, and honey drizzle
A white for guests who want richness with freshness
Wonderful on its own as the evening warms up
final: Old vines, new energy—generous and alive.

title: Alvarinho — Contacto
quickDescription: Salty, bright, and coastal-feeling — refreshing without being simple.
opening: A white that feels floral and cool at once—lively, aromatic, and just barely softer than bone dry.
colorDesc: Pale straw with glints of green and silver.
nose: White flowers, lime, peach skin, and cool minerality.
palate: Bright, lightly textured, and very fresh.
place: Minho, in Portugal's northwest corner, is one of Europe's greenest and most Atlantic-influenced wine regions.
producer: Contacto presents Alvarinho in a modern, pure style: aromatic, bright, and focused on preserving the grape's natural lift.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted something on the list that felt almost fragrant before it ever felt fruity. This wine has a kind of cool floral brightness that makes it memorable immediately.
note: We wanted something on the list that felt almost fragrant before it ever felt fruity. This wine has a kind of cool floral brightness that makes it memorable immediately.
pairings: Fresh cheeses, olives, and lighter boards
A white for spring afternoons and clean palates
Ideal for guests who want something elegant but not severe
final: Like walking through flowers in a cold breeze.

title: Gentil — Hugel
quickDescription: Friendly, aromatic, and versatile — an easy introduction to Alsace.
opening: A white blend that feels generous and aromatic—floral, softly textured, and easy to keep returning to.
colorDesc: Soft gold with a warm, inviting glow.
nose: White flowers, stone fruit, and spice.
palate: Rounded and aromatic with a gentle sweetness.
place: Alsace sits along France's eastern border and is known for highly aromatic whites. Its long growing season helps grapes retain fragrance.
producer: Hugel is one of Alsace's classic names, known for wines that introduce the region clearly and generously.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a white that felt expressive and welcoming. This is one of those wines that opens outward. It does not hide. It invites people in.
note: We wanted a white that felt expressive and welcoming. This is one of those wines that opens outward. It does not hide. It invites people in.
pairings: Boards with fruit preserve and creamy cheeses
An easy choice for guests who love aromatics
A soft landing in the middle of the white list
final: Aromatic, generous, and impossible to ignore.

title: Old Vine Chenin Blanc — Fleur du Cap
quickDescription: Textured and intriguing — a white with odd little corners and real personality.
opening: A white with tension and odd little corners—slightly sweet, slightly waxy, and very much its own thing.
colorDesc: Pale gold with a soft waxy glow.
nose: Orchard fruit, honeyed edges, and dried herbs.
palate: Textured and lightly sweet, balanced by freshness.
finish: Slightly savory, slightly sweet, and intriguing.
place: The Western Cape has become one of the world's great homes for Chenin Blanc. Old vines produce wines with both freshness and density.
producer: Fleur du Cap emphasizes expressive fruit and texture, showing how Chenin can be at once approachable and unexpectedly layered.
whyWeChoseIt: We chose this because every list needs one wine that makes people tilt their head and go back for another sip. It is weird in the best way.
note: We chose this because every list needs one wine that makes people tilt their head and go back for another sip. It is weird in the best way.
pairings: Fruit preserves, nuts, and creamy elements on the board
A conversation piece for curious drinkers
Best for guests who like wines with texture and surprise
final: A beautiful little oddball.

title: Gewürztraminer — Hyland Estate
quickDescription: Floral and aromatic, but balanced enough to stay elegant.
opening: An aromatic white that feels fragrant and bright—tropical, floral, and more lifted than heavy.
colorDesc: Soft gold with a luminous tropical sheen.
nose: Lychee, rose petal, and ripe tropical fruit.
palate: Lightly sweet and aromatic with a soft, perfumed texture.
place: McMinnville, in Oregon's Willamette Valley, can produce aromatic whites with freshness and poise.
producer: Hyland Estate works with cool-climate fruit that allows the wine's perfume to shine.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted an aromatic white on the list that felt bright and welcoming rather than dense or heavy. This one perfumes the glass without ever losing its balance.
note: We wanted a sweeter wine on the list that felt bright and welcoming rather than dense.
pairings: Fruit compote, soft cheeses, and lighter boards
A white for guests who love aromatics and perfume
A soft, joyful turn in the white list
final: Perfumed and playful to the last sip.

title: Riesling Armand Kabinett — Von Buhl
quickDescription: Light on its feet, quietly expressive, and built for people who love freshness.
opening: A wine that walks the line between sweetness and tension—bright, aromatic, and full of contrast.
colorDesc: Green-gold, luminous and slightly deeper than a dry white.
nose: Flowers, citrus, and a distinct petrol edge.
palate: Sweetness balanced by sharp acidity, keeping it lively and refreshing.
place: Germany produces some of the most expressive Rieslings in the world. Cool climates slow ripening and preserve acidity.
producer: Von Buhl embraces contrast—aromatic yet precise, expressive yet structured.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted something on the list that surprised people. This Riesling changes minds almost immediately—proof that off-dry can be thrilling.
note: We wanted something on the list that surprised people. This Riesling changes minds almost immediately.
pairings: Fresh fruit, lighter desserts, or nothing at all
Late in the evening, when things slow down
A conversation that turns reflective
final: A wine that proves contrast can be beautiful.

title: Mtsvane — Wine Thieves
quickDescription: Earthy, herbal, and adventurous — a white that genuinely widens the map.
opening: A white that feels sunbaked and herbal—dry, earthy, and completely unlike the usual route through a wine list.
colorDesc: Straw colored with a slightly rustic golden hue.
nose: Dried herbs, dusty earth, and preserved citrus.
palate: Dry, savory, and textured with a distinctly herbal profile.
place: Georgia is one of the oldest wine-producing regions in the world, with thousands of years of winemaking history.
producer: Wine Thieves brings a more adventurous, place-driven bottle to the table, introducing guests to a grape and region outside the usual conversation.
whyWeChoseIt: We chose this because we wanted the list to have one white that genuinely widens the map. It is not here to behave. It is here to say something.
note: We chose this because we wanted the list to have one white that genuinely widens the map. It is not here to behave. It is here to say something.
pairings: Olives, pickles, nuts, and salty cheeses
A bottle for adventurous tables
Best when someone says, "Give me something different."
final: A dry white with dust, history, and attitude.

title: Mâcon-Lugny — Cave de Lugny
quickDescription: Golden and comforting — the white wine equivalent of candlelight.
opening: A Chardonnay that feels like comfort with structure—golden, calm, and quietly luxurious.
colorDesc: Warm gold with rich, soft reflections.
nose: Baked apple, almond, butter, and pastry.
palate: Round and smooth with gentle richness.
place: Mâcon sits in southern Burgundy, where Chardonnay often shows a softer, more generous side than farther north.
producer: Cave de Lugny is known for approachable, classic white Burgundy that highlights Chardonnay's elegance.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one white on the list that felt like a deep exhale. This is that bottle. It is comforting, but still refined.
note: We wanted one white on the list that felt like a deep exhale. This is that bottle. It is comforting, but still refined.
pairings: Manchego, whipped feta, roasted peppers, and warm bread
A white for guests who want richness without excess
Excellent when the evening starts to settle in
final: The white wine equivalent of candlelight.

title: Morey-Saint-Denis — Domaine Arlaud
quickDescription: Serious but beautiful, with depth, poise, and old-world charm.
opening: A Pinot Noir that feels old-world and intimate—softly haunted, elegant, and full of memory.
colorDesc: Dusty ruby with a slightly weathered, translucent edge.
nose: Dried cherry, earth, old leather, and faded rose.
palate: Silky and savory with fine structure.
place: Morey-Saint-Denis is one of Burgundy's great Pinot villages, known for wines that balance perfume with earth.
producer: Domaine Arlaud farms biodynamically and builds wines around finesse rather than force, letting Pinot Noir express soil, age, and atmosphere in a quieter register.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a wine on the red list that felt almost literary. This is not a loud red. It rewards attention and mood. It belongs in this room.
note: We wanted a wine on the red list that felt almost literary. This is not a loud red. It rewards attention and mood. It belongs in this room.
pairings: Cured meats, chèvre, and slower evenings
A glass for guests who like subtlety over impact
Best when conversation lowers by a few decibels
final: Elegant, earthy, and full of lived-in charm.

title: Pinot Noir — Failla
quickDescription: Soft, graceful, and earthy — subtle in the best way.
opening: A Pinot with grace—bright fruit, cool-climate lift, and an emotional volume tuned just right.
colorDesc: Clear ruby with bright red highlights.
nose: Red berries, tea leaf, and forest floor.
palate: Juicy but balanced, with tension underneath the fruit.
place: The Willamette Valley is shaped by cool air and long growing seasons, allowing Pinot Noir to ripen slowly and retain its elegant freshness.
producer: Failla is known for wines with restraint and site expression, crafting Pinot Noir that stays graceful and grounded.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted to show two Pinots on the list for a reason. This one lets guests feel the contrast—brighter, more immediate, and more emotionally open.
note: We wanted to show two Pinots on the list for a reason. This one lets guests feel the contrast—brighter, more immediate, and more emotionally open.
pairings: Prosciutto, fruit preserve, and aged cheese
A red for guests who want vibrancy without heaviness
Perfect for comparing old world and new world side by side
final: Pinot turned up just enough.

title: Côtes du Rhône "Esprit" — Delas
quickDescription: Comforting and easygoing — a relaxed, everyday kind of Rhône red.
opening: A red that feels warm, familiar, and just a little unruly—in the way good table wines often are.
colorDesc: Medium ruby with warm cherry tones.
nose: Red fruit, herbs, spice, and sun-warmed earth.
palate: Soft, savory, and comfortably full without becoming heavy.
place: The Southern Rhône is a region of sun, herbs, stones, and blends. Wines here often feel naturally suited to food and conversation.
producer: Delas has long worked within the Rhône tradition, offering wines that feel rooted, savory, and made for sharing.
whyWeChoseIt: We chose this because every list needs one red that simply feels hospitable. It is the bottle that makes people relax into the evening.
note: We chose this because every list needs one red that simply feels hospitable. It is the bottle that makes people relax into the evening.
pairings: Salami, peppers, and anything savory on the board
An easy center-of-the-table red
The kind of wine that keeps getting poured
final: Warm, savory, and deeply shareable.

title: Rioja Reserva — Milenrama
quickDescription: Savory, polished, and quietly traditional — a red for lingering over.
opening: A wine that feels grounded—earthy, warm, and quietly confident in its place at the table.
colorDesc: Deep ruby with soft brick tones at the edge.
nose: Dried cherry, leather, tomato leaf, and warm spice.
palate: Smooth and balanced with gentle tannins and savory depth.
place: Rioja is one of Spain's most historic wine regions, known for wines that age gracefully.
producer: Milenrama leans into a traditional Rioja expression—letting time do the work.
whyWeChoseIt: Every list needs a wine that feels like it belongs at the center of the table. This is that wine for us.
note: Every list needs a wine that feels like it belongs at the center of the table. This is that wine for us.
pairings: Manchego, cured meats, and roasted vegetables
Long conversations that drift between topics
A second glass you did not plan on
final: A wine that does not try too hard—and does not need to.

title: Brunello di Montalcino
quickDescription: Serious, structured, and age-worthy — a red with real spine.
opening: A red with dust, structure, and seriousness—one of those wines that asks you to sit up a little straighter.
colorDesc: Brick red with warm rust tones around the rim.
nose: Dried cherry, cedar, leather, and warm earth.
palate: Firm, savory, and structured with notable grip.
place: Montalcino, in Tuscany, is one of Italy's great red wine villages. Brunello is known for combining acidity, structure, and age-worthy depth.
producer: This bottle speaks in a traditional register: less about plush fruit, more about structure, earth, and the slow unfolding that makes Brunello compelling.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one wine that felt unmistakably serious without becoming inaccessible. This bottle gives the list backbone.
note: We wanted one wine that felt unmistakably serious without becoming inaccessible. This bottle gives the list backbone.
pairings: Aged cheeses, salami, and longer conversations
A red for slower drinkers
Best when someone wants something more classical and composed
final: A wine with real spine.

title: Malbec "Terroir Único" — Zorzal
quickDescription: Fresh and mineral-driven, showing a more lifted side of Malbec.
opening: A Malbec with darkness and brightness at once—fruit-forward, juicy, and a little dramatic.
colorDesc: Deep purple-ruby with saturated color.
nose: Blackberry, plum, violet, and spice.
palate: Juicy and dark-fruited with smooth texture.
finish: Plush, vivid, and lightly tart at the edge.
place: Argentina has made Malbec one of the world's most recognizable reds, often balancing mountain-grown freshness with deep, dark fruit.
producer: Zorzal is known for fresher expressions that keep Malbec from becoming too heavy.
whyWeChoseIt: We chose this because not every dark wine on the list should feel serious or brooding. This one brings color, energy, and just enough tartness.
note: We chose this because not every dark wine on the list should feel serious or brooding. This one brings color, energy, and just enough tartness.
pairings: Calabrese salami, nuts, and fruit preserve
A red for guests who like richness with a pulse
Easy to love, but not one-note
final: Dark fruit with a flash of mischief.

title: Old Vine Zinfandel — Turley
quickDescription: Bold, dark-fruited, and generous — a red with warmth and personality.
opening: A red that feels bold, sunlit, and chewable—fruit-forward in the best old-vine way.
colorDesc: Dark ruby leaning garnet at the center.
nose: Blackberry jam, spice, and sun-dried fruit.
palate: Full, generous, and packed with concentrated fruit.
place: Northern California's old vine sites produce Zinfandel with concentration and personality.
producer: Turley has become one of the benchmark names for old-vine Zinfandel.
whyWeChoseIt: We chose this because sometimes a list needs one bottle that leans into pleasure unapologetically.
note: We chose this because sometimes a list needs one bottle that leans into pleasure unapologetically.
pairings: Aged cheeses, charcuterie, and savory-sweet pairings
A red for guests who want impact
The bottle that fills the room a little more
final: Big-hearted and gloriously concentrated.

title: Saperavi Barrel — Giuaani
quickDescription: Dark, earthy, and ancient — an inky red with real gravity.
opening: A red with darkness, spice, and old-world depth—earthy, inky, and beautifully stern.
colorDesc: Dark anise-red with inky depth.
nose: Dark fruit, tobacco, spice, and earth.
palate: Dry and structured with serious color and grip.
place: Kvareli sits in Georgia's Kakheti region, one of the oldest wine cultures in the world.
producer: Giuaani presents a strong regional identity here: less fruit-polish, more depth, spice, and earth.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one red that truly stretched the map and the palate. This bottle gives the list a darker, older, more rooted voice.
note: We wanted one red that truly stretched the map and the palate. This bottle gives the list a darker, older, more rooted voice.
pairings: Aged cheeses, salami, and slower pours
A red for guests who like structure and depth
Best when someone asks for something off the beaten path
final: An inky red with real gravity.

title: Bordeaux — Château St. Sulpice
quickDescription: Classic and structured, with enough fruit to stay welcoming.
opening: A red Bordeaux with poise and balance—dark-fruited, gently tannic, and unmistakably classic.
colorDesc: Deep garnet with ruby reflections at the edge.
nose: Dark plum, cedar, graphite, and subtle dried herbs.
palate: Smooth and structured with ripe dark fruit, polished tannins, and a savory undercurrent.
finish: Firm, clean, and lingering with quiet confidence.
place: Bordeaux remains one of the world's defining red wine regions—centuries of tradition, blended grapes, and a style built around structure, balance, and age.
producer: Château St. Sulpice delivers a classic Bordeaux expression at an approachable level: well-structured, fruit-forward enough to enjoy young, and polished enough to feel serious.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted a Bordeaux on the list that felt traditional without being intimidating. This one offers the structure and composure people expect from the region, while staying welcoming and warm.
note: We wanted a Bordeaux on the list that felt traditional without being intimidating. This one offers the structure and composure people expect from the region, while staying welcoming and warm.
pairings: Aged cheese, charcuterie, and roasted vegetables
A red for guests who want something classic and grounded
Feels right at the center of any table
final: Composed, welcoming, and quietly commanding.

title: Zinfandel Pagani Ranch — Ridge
quickDescription: Powerful, layered, and cellar-worthy — a statement red with depth.
opening: A full red that feels stocked, seasoned, and ready for colder weather—dense fruit with pantry depth.
colorDesc: Dark red with opaque fruit at the center.
nose: Black fruit, spice, cedar, and dried pantry notes.
palate: Rich, layered, and substantial without losing shape.
place: Sonoma County can produce Zinfandel with both concentration and structure, especially in benchmark sites.
producer: Ridge is one of California's iconic names, known for making powerful reds that still feel disciplined and grounded.
whyWeChoseIt: We chose this because the list needed one red that felt truly cellar-worthy—something bigger, deeper, and more commanding.
note: We chose this because the list needed one red that felt truly cellar-worthy—something bigger, deeper, and more commanding.
pairings: Hard cheeses, cured meats, and rich savory bites
A bottle for guests who want a statement red
Feels especially right in colder months
final: A pantry of dark fruit and spice, opened in winter.

title: Shiraz — Barossa Valley Estate
quickDescription: Dark, full, and expressive — the richest red on the page.
opening: A red with black-fruited confidence—peppered, plush, and unmistakably Barossa.
colorDesc: Deep plum-black with dark violet tones.
nose: Black plum, cracked pepper, and warm spice.
palate: Bold and smooth with dark fruit concentration.
place: Barossa Valley is one of Australia's most recognized red wine regions, producing Shiraz that combines generosity, ripeness, and spice.
producer: Barossa Valley Estate keeps the style classic: dark-fruited, peppery, and confidently full-bodied.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one bottle on the list that delivered immediate richness and spice. This is that wine.
note: We wanted one bottle on the list that delivered immediate richness and spice. This is that wine.
pairings: Salami, manchego, and bold savory boards
A red for guests who want fullness without fuss
Comforting in a broad-shouldered way
final: A dark red with warmth and swagger.

title: Cabernet Sauvignon — Faust
quickDescription: Cabernet with confidence: deep fruit, structure, and a long finish.
opening: A Cabernet that feels structured and generous at once—dark fruit, firm shape, and unmistakable presence.
colorDesc: Opaque blackberry-black with a firm, saturated core.
nose: Blackberry, cassis, cedar, and dark spice.
palate: Structured and full with broad dark fruit and polished tannins.
place: Napa Valley has become one of the world's defining homes for Cabernet Sauvignon.
producer: Faust offers a polished Napa expression that stays generous without losing shape.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one Cabernet that felt worthy of its place at the top of the red list.
note: We wanted one Cabernet that felt worthy of its place at the top of the red list.
pairings: Aged cheese, cured meats, and the richer end of the board
A red for guests who know they want Cabernet
Built for slower, more deliberate sipping
final: A strong closing argument for the red list.

title: 10 Year Tawny Port — Dow
quickDescription: Nutty, mellow, and softly sweet — a beautiful way to end.
opening: A sweet wine that feels burnished and complete—warm, nutty, and made for the slowest part of the night.
colorDesc: Clay-red amber with warm tawny edges.
nose: Raisin, toasted nuts, caramel, and dried fig.
palate: Sweet, rich, and mellow with oxidative complexity.
place: Portugal's fortified wines carry centuries of history. Tawny Port develops character through years of aging in the Douro Valley.
producer: Dow is one of the classic Port houses, known for wines with concentration, balance, and refined sweetness.
whyWeChoseIt: We wanted one ending on the list that felt unmistakably final—in the best possible sense. This Port closes the evening with warmth.
note: We wanted one ending on the list that felt unmistakably final—in the best possible sense. This Port closes the evening with warmth.
pairings: Chocolate tart bite, biscotti crumble, and candied citrus
The last glass of the evening
A dessert wine for lingering
final: The kind of wine that teaches people to stay a little longer.
